content.full_text:
January 13, 1994


Honorable Ken Yorde
City Councilmember
City of Rocklin
3970 Rocklin Road
P.O. Box 1138
Rocklin, CA  95677

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-93-486

Dear Councilmember Yorde:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing your responsibilities as a Rocklin City Councilmember under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.  In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.  The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

                                        QUESTIONS

     Do you have a conflict of interest in the following decision
by virtue of your ownership of 10 acres of undeveloped property in
the Sunset Ranchos Estates:

     1.  The possible annexation of an area including your
property by the city.

     2.  The consideration and adoption of the North Rocklin
Circulation Element Project which includes projects near your
property.

     3.  The potential rezoning of the Stanford Ranch area from
industrial to residential.  The project site is 4,000 feet from
your property.

_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5560

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. Because the annexation of the Sunset Ranchos Area would include your property, the effect of the decision is deemed to be material and you may not participate in the decision.

2. To the extent that you have a conflict of interest with respect to any component of the North Rocklin Circulation Element, you must disqualify yourself with respect to the adoption of the project as a whole.

However, if the other component decisions are separable from those for which you have a conflict of interest (as discussed below), you may participate in the other components so long as the decisions in which you have a disqualifying financial interest are segregated from the other decisions and a final decision reached on them by the city council without your participation. Once a decision has been made on the portions of the plan for which you have a disqualifying interest, you may participate in the deliberations regarding other portions of the plan, so long as those deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which you were disqualified.

3. You may participate in the decision whether to rezone the Stanford Ranch area so long as: (1) the decision will not affect the value of your real property by $10,000 or more, or the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period; or, (2) the effect on your property will be substantially the same as the effect on 25 percent of all the properties within a 2,500 foot radius of your property, and there are at least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot radius of your property.

FACTS

On November 2, 1993, you were elected to the Rocklin City Council. You and your spouse own a home in the jurisdiction, located at 4335 Midas Avenue, Rocklin.

You also own a ten-acre parcel of undeveloped land located in the unincorporated territory of Placer County, but within the City of Rocklin's sphere of influence. The property is part of a 137 lot subdivision known as Sunset Ranchos Estates Unit 1. Your lot is located in the northern most portion of the subdivision, immediately adjacent to the outer boundary of Rocklin's sphere of influence and approximately 4,000 feet from the city's border.

The land is currently zoned for one unit per ten acres (Placer County). The present value of your lot is over $100,000. There are more than 33 separate parcels of land within 2,500 feet of your lot owned by 20 separate owners.

The City of Rocklin will soon be considering a variety of issues and development proposals.

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 3


     o  The Sunset Ranchos Annexation.  The city may receive an
annexation and development application for the Sunset Ranchos
areas.  This application may include your parcel.

     You stated that in 1992, you authorized a third party to
represent you and include your lot in the annexation and
development proposal which was submitted to the City of Rocklin
for pre-application review.  A pre-application meeting was held
with the city staff on the proposal, but no formal application has
been filed.

     Since your election to the city council, you terminated the
authorization to include your lot.  It is your understanding that
under state law, your lot may still be included in the annexation
proposal without your authorization or participation.

     o  The Stanford Ranch Phase IV Modification.  The city is
processing an amendment to a development project known as Stanford
Ranch Phase IV.  The proposal is to rezone industrially zoned land
to residentially zoned land.  The land is currently undeveloped.
The project site is located approximately 4,000 feet from your
property.

     o  The City of Rocklin is also preparing a project known as
the North Rocklin Circulation Element.  The project consists of
the revisions of the circulation element of the Rocklin General
Plan as it relates to an area of the city that includes over 85
percent of the total land area of the city.  The project also
covers portions of the unincorporated territory within the city's
sphere of influence, including the area where your lot is located.

     You stated that a chief objective of the project is to design
a circulation system which will accommodate development within the
city and its sphere of influence to the year 2020.

     The project has two components.  The first is the Base
Circulation Plan which would accommodate the city's projected
growth to the year 2020.  The improvements are recommended to
resolve anticipated level of service problems due to the projected
growth; to reduce traffic on certain key collector roadways with
residential frontage; and to improve circulation in some high
growth areas of the city.  None of the proposed improvements are
located in the vicinity of your property.  However, approval of
the Base Circulation Plan, which would improve the overall
circulation system in the city, would enhance the development
potential of your property.

     The second component of the project is the Long Range
Planning Options.  This component was included in case the city
allowed higher densities in areas within its "planning reserve"
(sphere of influence), such as the Sunset Ranchos.  It is not
anticipated that these improvements would be needed before the
year 2020.  These proposed improvements are located much closer to
the property (probably within 2,500 feet).  While the current
zoning in the Sunset Ranchos area allows for a development density
of one unit per ten acres, this project assumes a higher

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 4


development density of one unit per acre, anticipating that an
increase in density is likely before development occurs.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision which will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth $1,000 or more.² (Section 87103(b).)

You stated that you own real property near the boundaries of
the City of Rocklin. Consequently, you may not participate in a
governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable³
material financial effect on your real property.

The standard for materiality differs depending on whether
your real property is directly or indirectly involved in the
decision. Generally, if your real property is directly involved
in a decision, the effect or the decision is deemed to be material
and disqualification is required. In all other cases, materiality
is determined by applying an indirect effect test pursuant to
Regulation 18702.3.⁴

Sunset Ranchos Annexation

Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) provides a list of situations in
which an official's property is directly involved in a decision.
They include decisions to zone or rezone the official's property,
decisions to annex the property, decisions concerning assessments
on the property and decisions to include the property in or
exclude it from any city, county or other local governmental
subdivision.


2
You described your undeveloped lot and your personal residence
in your letter. However, you did not describe facts that would
suggest that your residence is near any of the projects or will be
affected by the projects. To the extent that your residence is
near any of the projects, you would apply the same rules discussed
with respect to your undeveloped lots.

3
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

4
With respect to a leasehold interest in real property,
Regulation 18702.4 would apply.

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 5


You stated that a proposal to annex the Sunset Ranchos Area
to the city may come before the city council in the future. This
application may include your property, however, you stated that
you are not the applicant, nor are you represented by the
applicant.

However, Regulation 18702.1 provides:

(a) The effect of a decision is material if
any of the following applies:

* * *

(3) Interest in Real Property—

(A) The decision involves the
zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease,
or inclusion in or exclusion from any
city, county, district or other local
governmental subdivision, of real
property in which the official has a
direct or indirect interest (other than a
leasehold interest) of $1,000 or more, or
a similar decision affecting such
property.

Thus, irrespective of your connection to the application, the
effect of the decision is deemed to be material because it
involves the annexation of your property.5 Thus, you may not
participate in the decision.

North Rocklin Circulation Element Project

With respect to the amendment to the circulation element of
the city's general plan, your property would not be directly
involved in the decision. Thus, your property would be indirectly
involved in the decision.

As noted earlier, Regulation 18702.3 provides standards to
determine whether the foreseeable financial effect of a decision
will be material as to an official's real property. Regulation
18702.3(a) sets out three tests that are generally applied.

(1) The effect of a decision is material if the official
owns property within 300 feet of property that is the subject of a
decision and the decision will have some financial effect on the
property, or if the decision involves construction of, or

5 Even if your property were not included in the application, you
still may have a conflict of interest with respect to the
annexation decision since the property that will be the subject of
the decision is in proximity to property you own. (See e.g.,
Strauss Advice Letter, No. A-90-134, copy enclosed.) You should
contact us for further advice should this contingency occur.

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 6


improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar
facilities, and the official's property will receive new or
substantially improved services.

     (2) Where the official's real property is beyond a radius of
300 feet from the subject property, but within a 2,500 feet, the
effect of a decision will be material only if the decision will
affect the value of the official's property by $10,000 or more, or
the rental value of the property by $1,000 in a 12 month period.

     (3) Finally, if the official's property is located beyond a
2,500 foot radius of the subject property, the effect of a
decision is material only if there are specific circumstances
regarding the decision which make it foreseeable that the decision
will affect the value of the official's property by $10,000 or
more, or the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12
month period.  However, even if the decision has such an effect on
the property, the effect is not deemed material if the effect on
the official's property will be substantially the same as the
effect upon at least 25 percent of all the properties within a
2,500 foot radius of the official's property, and there are at
least 10 properties under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot
radius of the official's property.

     Thus, under Regulation 18702.3(a)(1), each decision must be
examined independently to determine if you have a conflict of
interest. Where other decisions are separable, the following
procedure should be followed:

     (1) The decisions in which you have a disqualifying
financial interest should be segregated from the other decisions.

     (2) The decisions from which you are disqualified should be
considered first, and a final decision reached by the city council
without your participation.

     (3) Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
plan for which you have a disqualifying interest, you may
participate in the deliberations regarding other portions of the
plan, so long as those deliberations do not result in a reopening
or in any way affect the decisions from which you were
disqualified. (Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)

     However, under some circumstances a series of decisions may
be too interrelated to be considered separately. (Miller Advice
Letter, No. A-82-119.)  For example, if a decision concerning a
freeway is presented to the city council as an alternative to a
decision for which you are disqualified, you could not be able to
participate in any of the decisions. (Nord Advice Letter, No. A-
82-038.)  This is because the determination of the freeway
decision would effectively determine the results of the decisions
for which you are disqualified.  Thus, where the decisions are too
interrelated to be decided separately, you must disqualify
yourself as to all the decisions under consideration. (Scher
Advice Letter, No. A-88-479.)

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 7


Consistent with this reasoning, to the extent that you have a
conflict of interest with respect to any component of the plan,
you would also be disqualified from adopting the plan as a whole.

     1. Projects within 2,500 Feet of Your Property

     You stated that some of the future projects affect property
within 2,500 feet of your property. Consequently, you may not
participate in a decision regarding these projects if the decision
could foreseeably increase or decrease the fair market value of
your real property by $10,000 or more or the rental value of your
property by at least $1,000 in a 12-month period. (Regulation
18702.3(a)(3)(A).)

     We cannot determine whether a project will have such an
effect on your real property; we must leave this factual
determination to you and your city attorney. However, Regulation
18702.3(d) sets forth factors that must be considered in
determining whether a decision will have a material financial
effect on your real property.6

     A. The proximity of the property which is the subject of the
decision and the magnitude of the proposed project or change in
use in relationship to the property in which the official has an
interest;

     B. Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will affect the development potential or income producing
potential of the property;

     C. Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will result in a change to the character of the neighborhood
including, but not limited to, the effect on traffic, view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar
traits of the neighborhood.

     2. Projects Beyond 2,500 Feet of Your Property

     However, you stated that none of the proposed improvements
were within the vicinity of your real property. We assume from
your facts that the specific improvements are beyond 2,500 feet of
your property. However, you also stated that the overall goal of
the plan was to accommodate future growth and accommodate the
development potential of your property. If it is foreseeable that
the decision will affect the value of your real property by
$10,000 or more, or the rental value of the property by $1,000 or
more per 12 month period, you will be required to disqualify
yourself from the decision.

     However, Regulation 18702.3(b) provides that even if the
decision has such an effect on the property, the effect is not
deemed material if:

_________________________
     6 The factors to be considered are not limited to the factors
specified in Regulation 18702.3(d).

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 8


A.  The effect on your property will be substantially the
same as the effect on 25 percent of all the properties within a
2,500 foot radius of your property, and

B.  There are at least 10 properties under separate ownership
within a 2,500 foot radius of your property.

You stated that there are more than 33 separate parcels owned
by 33 separate property owners within 2,500 feet of your property.
If 25 percent of these properties will be affected in
substantially the same manner as your property, you may
participate in the decisions.  Of course, whether the effect on
your property will be substantially the same as the effect on 25
percent of the property owners within 2,500 feet of your property
is a factual determination that the Commission cannot make.
However, factors that should be considered include whether the
neighboring properties are developed or undeveloped and the
relative size of the properties.  Since you own 10 acres of
undeveloped property, it would appear that only persons owning
approximately the same amount of property would be affected in the
same manner.

3.  "Public Generally" Exception

Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act if the effect on the official's
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.

On September 7, 1993, the Commission adopted specific
standards to determine when a governmental decision will fall
within the "public generally" exception.  New Regulation 18703
provides that the "public generally" exception applies where both
subdivisions (1) and (2) apply.

(1)  Significant Segment:  The governmental
decision will affect a "significant segment" of the
public generally as set forth below:

(A)  The decision will affect:

(i)  Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

(ii)  Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

(iii)  Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents, so long

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 9

as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,

(B) The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,

* * *

(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The
governmental decision will affect the official's
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision (a)(1) of this
regulation.

Thus, with respect to any of the decisions discussed above,
if the decisions fit within the "public generally" exception, you
may participate. Please note, however, that application of the
exception turns on whether the governmental decision will affect
your economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the economic interests of a significant segment of the
public generally (as discussed above). Since you own 10 acres of
undeveloped property, it would appear that only persons owning
approximately the same amount of property would be affected in the
same manner. Conversely, with respect to your residence the
exception may apply under more circumstances.

Stanford Ranch Phase IV Modification

As discussed above, Regulation 18702.3(b) provides that if a
project is more than 2,500 feet from your real property, you will
be required to disqualify yourself from decisions affecting the
project if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
affect the value of your real property by $10,000 or more, or the
rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period. However, if the decision has such an effect on the
property, disqualification will not be required if:

A. The effect on your property will be substantially the
same as the effect on 25 percent of all the properties within a
2,500 foot radius of your property, and

B. There are at least 10 properties under separate ownership
within a 2,500 foot radius of your property.

As we stated above, whether the effect on your property will
be substantially the same as the effect on 25 percent of the
property owners within 2,500 feet of your property is a factual
determination that the Commission cannot make.

Our File No. A-93-486
Page 10


If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures




7 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

QUESTION: Do you have a conflict of interest in the following decision
by virtue of your ownership of 10 acres of undeveloped property in
the Sunset Ranchos Estates:
1.
The possible annexation of an area including your
property by the city.
2.
The consideration and adoption of the North Rocklin
Circulation Element Project which includes projects near your
property.
3.
The potential rezoning of the Stanford Ranch area from
industrial to residential.
The project site is 4,000 feet from
your property.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—93-486
•

CONCLUSION: 1.
Because the annexation of the Sunset Ranchos Area would
include your property, the effect of the decision is deemed to be
material and you may not participate in the decision.
2.
To the extent that you have a conflict of interest with
respect to any component of the North Rocklin Circulation Element,
you must disqualify yourself with respect to the adoption of the
project as a whole.
However, if the other component decisions are separable from
those for which you have a conflict of interest (as discussed
below),
yàu may participate in the other components so long as the
decisions in which you have a disqualifying financial interest are
segregated from the other decisions and a final decision reached
on them by the city council without your participation.
Once a
decision has been made on the portions of the plan for which you
have a disqualifying interest, you may participate in the
deliberations regarding other portions of the plan,
so long as
those deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way
affect the decisions from which you were disqualified.
3.
You may participate in the decision whether to rezone the
Stanford Ranch area so long as:
(1)
the decision will not affect
the value of your real property by $10,000 or more,
or the rental
value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period; or,
(2)
the effect on your property will be substantially the same as
the effect on 25 percent of all the properties within a 2,500 foot
radius of your property,
and there are at least 10 properties
under separate ownership within a 2,500 foot radius of your
property.
embedding.first_500_words: January 13, 1994 Honorable Ken Yorde City Councilmember City of Rocklin 3970 Rocklin Road P.O. Box 1138 Rocklin, CA 95677 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-93-486 Dear Councilmember Yorde: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard- ing your responsibilities as a Rocklin City Councilmember under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS Do you have a conflict of interest in the following decision by virtue of your ownership of 10 acres of undeveloped property in the Sunset Ranchos Estates: 1. The possible annexation of an area including your property by the city. 2. The consideration and adoption of the North Rocklin Circulation Element Project which includes projects near your property. 3. The potential rezoning of the Stanford Ranch area from industrial to residential. The project site is 4,000 feet from your property. _________________________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5560 Our File No. A-93-486 Page 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. Because the annexation of the Sunset Ranchos Area would include your property, the effect of the decision is deemed to be material and you may not participate in the decision. 2. To the extent that you have a conflict of interest with respect to any component of the North Rocklin Circulation Element, you must disqualify yourself with respect to the adoption of the project as a whole. However, if the other component decisions are separable from those for which you have a conflict of interest (as discussed below), you may participate in the other components so long as the decisions in which you have a disqualifying financial interest are segregated from the other decisions and a final decision reached on them by the city council without your participation. Once a decision has been made on the portions of the plan for which you have a disqualifying interest, you may participate in the deliberations regarding other portions of the plan, so long as those deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which you were disqualified. 3. You may participate in the decision whether to rezone the Stanford Ranch area so long as: (1) the decision will not affect the value of your real property by $10,000 or more, or the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month period; or, (2) the effect on your property
